title: Sheep handling pen layout and construction
subtitle: Building a basic race, footbath, and sorting pen from hurdles or timber.
excerpt: A handling system doesn't need to be permanent or elaborate to work well. A race that a single person can run sheep through, a footbath they can't avoid, and a small sorting pen where you can draft animals into two groups covers most routine smallholder husbandry. Here's how to lay it out and what you need to build it.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Sheep. SAC Consulting Scotland, Sheep Handling Facilities. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, sheep husbandry chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: A narrow passage just wide enough for one sheep, used to guide sheep through treatments such as footbathing or drenching.
A portable section of light metal or timber fencing used to create temporary pens and races.
A shallow trough containing a disinfectant or zinc-sulphate solution through which sheep are walked to treat or prevent foot problems.

content.text: Most smallholder sheep work, drenching, footbathing, crutching, tagging, weighing, requires you to move animals in a controlled way without a second person. A well-designed
and sorting pen lets one person do all of this in an hour rather than a full afternoon of chasing. The layout below is the minimum useful system for a flock of up to 30 sheep.

The system runs: gathering pen (large) → race (narrow, 4,6 m long) → footbath (optional, 1 m section at the end of the race) → drafting gate → two small pens for sorted animals. The gathering pen needs to hold your whole flock comfortably. The race needs to be just wide enough for one sheep and no wider, 45 cm is the standard for most British breeds.
45 cm apart, facing each other and wired together at the ends. Six hurdles on each side gives a 5 m race.
Stake the hurdles firmly at 1 m intervals. A sheep pushing sideways against a loose hurdle pushes the whole race out of line.
Fit a swing gate at the far end of the race that directs animals either left or right into the two sorting pens. This is the drafting gate, the single most useful part of the system.

sits in the race about halfway along, sunken to floor level so the sheep can't step over it. Minimum depth is 5 cm of solution; the bath needs to be long enough that each sheep takes at least two steps through it. Zinc sulphate (10%) is the standard preventive solution; formalin is effective but hazardous in an enclosed space and is less commonly used now.

A temporary hurdle system is adequate for most smallholders, but siting it in the same place for each use means the ground becomes a mud trap. Concrete or hardcore under the race and footbath area saves significant time in cleaning and makes the sheep easier to move. If you're planning a permanent installation, a curved race (the funnel design) moves sheep more efficiently than a straight one, sheep prefer to walk in a gentle curve than a straight line.
